region_of_origin: South Asia (India); widely used in Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for curry leaf as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of curry leaves is one sprig of approximately 10 fresh leaves, weighing about 2 g, used as a tempering herb in curries, dals, and rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Leaves, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo / Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, and iron taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fresh curry leaves. Secondary nutrient values (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat, potassium) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for curry leaves (NDB No. 02054 / FDC ID 170921) where HPB does not report the field. All values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Herbs — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate aromatic leaf category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for curry leaf (Murraya koenigii) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Curry leaf has very low carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100 g) with high dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g), yielding negligible glycemic impact. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate aromatic leaves and herbs documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 2 g serving = (15 × 2.4 / 100) × (2 / 100) = 0.01, which is nutritionally negligible. This estimation is conservative and clinically appropriate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (15 mg/100 g) and high potassium (450 mg/100 g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio for blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Curry leaves are suitable for individuals with hypertension; their very low sodium content (15 mg/100 g) and high potassium content (450 mg/100 g) support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and their dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g) contributes to vascular health, though culinary quantities consumed are small.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (2.4 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g), and carbazole alkaloids with evidence of improving insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism
type2_diabetes.guidance: Curry leaves are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their low carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.5 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g), and bioactive carbazole alkaloids (mahanimbine, girinimbine) have demonstrated insulin-sensitising and glucose-lowering effects in preclinical studies, making them a beneficial culinary herb for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), absence of trans fat, high dietary fibre, and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (carbazole alkaloids, flavonoids) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Curry leaves are suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; their low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), confirmed absence of trans fat, high dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g), and rich polyphenol content (carbazole alkaloids, flavonoids) contribute anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating effects, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (carbazole alkaloids, flavonoids) and vitamin C; negligible saturated fat and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Curry leaves are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; their anti-inflammatory carbazole alkaloids and flavonoids, combined with negligible saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) and absence of trans fat, present no pro-inflammatory dietary risk and may contribute modest anti-inflammatory benefit at culinary quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry leaf (Murraya koenigii) is a fresh aromatic herb native to South Asia, widely used in Indian, Sri Lankan, Singaporean Mamak, and Malaysian cuisines. The leaves are small, dark green, oval-shaped, and firm in texture, with a distinctively pungent, citrusy-herbal aroma and flavour that is released when fried in hot oil during tempering. Curry leaves are not the same as curry powder; they are a whole botanical leaf used fresh or dried. In Singaporean and Malaysian Mamak cooking, they are a foundational tempering ingredient added to hot oil alongside mustard seeds, dried chillies, and onions at the start of curry, dal, and rice preparations. Nutritionally, curry leaves are low in carbohydrates (2.4 g/100 g), moderate in protein (4.15 g/100 g), and contain a notable fat content (10.95 g/100 g) primarily from plant-based lipids. They are rich in dietary fibre (6.4 g/100 g), very low in sodium (15 mg/100 g), and high in potassium (450 mg/100 g). Their bioactive carbazole alkaloids — including mahanimbine and girinimbine — have demonstrated insulin-sensitising, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical research. Curry leaves are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Culinary serving sizes are small (approximately 2 g per sprig), so absolute nutrient contribution per meal is modest, but bioactive compound delivery is meaningful.
common_user_queries: Are curry leaves the same as curry powder?
Are curry leaves good for blood pressure?
What do curry leaves taste like?
How are curry leaves used in cooking?
What is the glycemic index of curry leaves?
meal_context: tempering base for curries and dals

ingredient_synonyms.Murraya koenigii: botanical name for curry leaf plant
ingredient_synonyms.sweet neem: alternate English name (not to be confused with bitter neem)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat, potassium) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for curry leaves. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate aromatic herbs, clearly documented. All ontology codes are defensible. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean Mamak cuisine context.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for curry leaves is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this food as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for curry leaves is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry used as primary source; USDA FoodData Central reports approximately 63.8 g/100 g water for raw curry leaves (FDC ID 170921) but this value was not incorporated to avoid mixing source precision levels — follow-up recommended. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo primary source entry for curry leaves; USDA FoodData Central partial data exists but was not incorporated as a mixed-source entry without explicit cross-validation. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170921) and consistent with the plant-based nature of this food. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0: naturally zero for a plant-based leaf food; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0: naturally zero for a plant-based food. 7. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Murraya koenigii leaves exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 8. dietary_tags 'low_calorie' and 'keto' from input preserved in cultural context; 'keto' tag is defensible given 2.4 g net carbohydrate per 100 g. 9. FoodEx2 code A00LC assigned as the closest defensible match for curry leaf as a fresh aromatic herb/leaf; reviewers should verify against the latest FoodEx2 browser release.
